body:
Hello, let's add riscv64 architecture target to the workflows using [RISE RISC-V Runners](https://riseproject-dev.github.io/riscv-runner/) (GitHub repository add-on for self-hosted runner-like access). I am using these runners now for many purposes and do observe them to be reliable and the organizers are responsive to support tickets and external contributions. The runner hardware is objectively low-end range of performance by modern standards but there is nothing to be gained going to qemu or cross-compile, nor waiting for faster hardware as there is demand now for this and collectively cpu cycles are saved by having compiled wheels available for users.

[cibuildwheel v3.1.2](https://github.com/pypa/cibuildwheel/releases/tag/v3.1.2) and PyPI pypi/warehouse#18243 enabled-by-default support for riscv64 is a recent happening in the past year. The build and use of riscv64 architecture is well-established many years before that. Now it is a current topic of interest to chase down all the dependencies to encourage publishing compiled wheels for riscv64 architecture :-)

I will submit a PR with the workflow changes however the discussion here is to track any PRs, a reference for downstream RISC-V users of Cython, and tracking any discussion about whether a 3rd party runner is allowed or if we should take another look at QEMU or cross-compile method.

Thanks! -E

comment_data.body:
We're in favour of Cython working across a broad range of platforms so would definitely accept bug reports to fix RISC-V issues.

However, Cython itself runs perfectly fine without being compiled, and we do distribute a pure Python wheel. Therefore I'm not sure that actually building Cython for RISC-V is worthwhile.

To be a bit clearer: if you do `pip install cython` on RISC-V right now, I'd expect it to install the `cython-3.2.4-py3-none-any.whl`. That should be quick to install (i.e. it's just unzipping and not compiling Cython).

The advantage of using compiled wheels is that Cython itself runs ~25% faster. It makes no difference to the speed of the modules that Cython produces.

Yes, this performance benefit is utilized for CI/CD workflows that depend on Cython. More of a benefit for me is to convince maintainers to build and publish their project's wheels from CI/CD workflow and not have any question about their upstream dependency i.e. Cython being less-than-optimal in those workflows.

My personal goal overall and how I discover Cython is in the service of readying Home Assistant for RISC-V architectural decision record to add official support; there it would not matter anyways if compiled Cython is published to PyPI because Home Assistant compiles and hosts a shadow repository of everything for the deployed user environment. However, it is counter to my goal if I am not able to show consistency of upstream adoption with pre-built wheels in the dependency chain, and the discussion with authors of those projects and with the discussion surrounding the ADR being affected is of concern for me.

What I propose for Cython does lean into RISE RISC-V runners being enabled in the GitHub apps which may be unfamiliar and so not be desirable. I do not fully understand the security model or threats to GitHub projects for this "add-ons" mechanism. However where it concerns seeking a comparable large scale community project using this RISE RISC-V Runner add-on, I can share that the Rust (programming language) project depends on the RISE RISC-V runners to build the Tier 2 host tools support for the riscv64gc-unknkown-linux-gnu target and there seems to be a positive long-term outlook from that team managing CI/CD resources;  The response from that team about promoting another supported RISC-V target without host tools into being a supported target with host tools (and so adding to the workflows) is that it is fine if limited to build on the well-known RISE RISC-V Runner so bypassing the general CI/CD infrastructure then it is not of any concern. Further as for sharing an opinion about my interactions with the RISC-V Runner administration team I've discovered a missing package as compared to official GitHub Actions runners and the subsequent pull request and issue filed was dispatched promptly and professionally. I could recommend this from personal experience. Though I don't know enough to comment on GitHub add-ons as a risk assessment, and obviously this is a community sponsored resource that runs on professionally managed low-end performance hardware;  it is objectively low performance, with good reliability, and some security-critical community projects do rely on it ... take that for whatever you would.

If you are going to consider build and publish for riscv64 architecture specific wheels then I also would say if there's not already qemu in your workflow don't also bother to add it for this situation. If something should interrupt access to the RISE runners just rip out the RISC-V stuff and have that written down as a social contract before doing this, maybe allowing some period of time for interested persons in the community to address concerns, but this should not be additional burden on the maintainers of Cython.

I'll submit a PR with the expectation that what I'm sending has been tested with the RISE RISC-V Runners to save you the headache of figuring out if this is worth your time from some rand-o person asking for RISC-V architecture specific builds using that resource. If you do want this as a qemu build in the workflows that would also further my own goals and I'm happy to figure that out.

For most projects, Cython is a build-time dependency. If your interest is in making package maintainers/distributors build more packages for RISC-V, then they can make use of Cython for their builds without Cython being specifically compiled (or in some cases even running on) RISC-V.

I can understand that platforms that commonly build their wheels on QEMU emulated CPUs would benefit even more than native ones from accelerated builds. But my impression is that even for them, the C compile times dominate the build times, not so much the Cython runs. GCC is really slow on QEMU.

We _could_ provide an abi3 wheel for Linux/RISC-V, but that would probably be less than 25% faster. And it would slow down the wheel build on our side.

My recommendation is: don't bother convincing us as long as there are loads of other interesting packages to bring to the platform. It'll be much easier to make us add a wheel target if you can motivate that Cython makes up a significant portion of the overall build times for RISC-V.

title: Build RISC-V wheels
